macro_segments.title: Opening remarks and review of the Mishna's list of exclusions from shaving

micro_segments.title: Introduction and overview of the daf
Review of categories of tumah where a Nazir does not shave: rabbinical vs. Torah-level exclusions
Overhanging trees and outcropping walls as examples of rabbinical tumah

macro_segments.title: Beis Hapras and the tumah of outside lands (Eretz Ha'amim)

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Beis Hapras and Eretz Ha'amim as categories of tumah
The Gemara's question: is Eretz Ha'amim's tumah due to the air or the ground?
Explanation of Avira and Gusha and their implications for tumah transmission

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's explanation of why the tumah of Eretz Ha'amim applies to airspace and its theological purpose

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's cases of being transported in a box, wagon, or boat in Eretz Ha'amim
The principle that Eretz Ha'amim's tumah is stricter than normal ohel laws
The purpose behind the tumah: to prevent Jews from leaving Eretz Yisrael

macro_segments.title: Analysis of the alternative interpretation: tumah based on ground (Gusha) and the role of dead bodies in Eretz Ha'amim

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's discussion of dead bodies as the source of tumah in Eretz Ha'amim
Historical and practical challenges to the gusha interpretation
The underlying policy rationale applies to both avira and gusha interpretations

macro_segments.title: Kavod HaAretz and the principle connecting Nazir shaving to Kohen warnings

micro_segments.title: The principle that what does not trigger Nazir shaving does not trigger Kohen warnings
Application to Eretz Ha'amim and the broader implications

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's resolution: Eretz Ha'amim tumah is avira-based and does not require sprinkling on days three and seven

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's proof from the requirement of sprinkling on days three and seven
Conclusion that if Eretz Ha'amim tumah were avira-based, sprinkling would not apply

macro_segments.title: Detailed analysis of vessels touching a corpse (Keilim Ha'nogim B'Meis) and the principle of Cherev Ke'Chalal

micro_segments.title: Why the Gemara assumes vessels are not subject to sprinkling on days three and seven
Tosafot's question: why not interpret Keilim as metal vessels under Cherev Ke'Chalal?
Rabbeinu Tam's position: if Cherev Ke'Chalal applied, a Nazir would shave

macro_segments.title: Rabbeinu Chaim HaKohen's response: the impossibility of applying Cherev Ke'Chalal strictly to metal vessels

micro_segments.title: Rabbeinu Chaim's letter to Rabbeinu Tam using the metaphor of a house for a Kohen
The problem of metal vessels in any house ever being in contact with a corpse
Conclusion that even metal vessels are not subject to Cherev Ke'Chalal for Nazir shaving

macro_segments.title: The Rambam's broader position on Cherev Ke'Chalal: all vessels, not just metal, but Nazir does not shave

micro_segments.title: Rambam's teaching that all vessels touched by a corpse are like the corpse itself
Rambam's derivation of Cherev Ke'Chalal from scripture
Summary of three positions: metal only, all vessels but Nazir does not shave, or Rambam's inclusive view

macro_segments.title: Return to Eretz Ha'amim and the tannaitic debate over airspace vs. ground via the case of one transported in a box

micro_segments.title: The Gemara presents a Tannaic debate: Rabbi (Judah HaNasi) vs. Rabbi Yosei bar Rabbi Yehuda on tumah of one in a box
Initial interpretation: the debate concerns whether avira or gusha is the source of Eretz Ha'amim's tumah
Alternative: both agree on gusha but disagree whether a transported box constitutes an ohel zaruk

macro_segments.title: The Baraita on one entering Eretz Ha'amim via wagon, boat, and mast: defining normal vs. unusual entry

micro_segments.title: The conceptual problem with applying 'lo shechiach' to a status rather than to prohibited actions
Baraita distinguishes box (tahor) from wagon, boat, and mast (tamei)
Analysis: the only difference is normalcy of entry method, suggesting avira-based tumah only for normal cases

macro_segments.title: Third possibility: gusha-based tumah with concern for leaning out of the box

micro_segments.title: Suggestion that tumah may be gusha-based but with presumption that one will lean out
Rabbi Yosei bar Rabbi Yehuda's position: tumah only upon actual leaning out (head or majority of body)
Summary of multiple interpretive possibilities and their implications

topical_tags.term: Avira vs. Gusha (air vs. ground as source of tumah)
Ohel and Ohel Zaruk (structure/tent status and moving structures)
Shabbat/Shlishi V'Shvii (sprinkling on days three and seven)
Cherev Ke'Chalal (sword treated as corpse; vessels as source of tumah)
Nazir megaleach (Nazir shaving head due to tumah)
Keilim ha'nogim b'meis (vessels touching a corpse)
Policy rationale: preventing emigration from Eretz Yisrael
Rabbeinu Tam (Tosafist position on Cherev Ke'Chalal)
Rabbeinu Chaim HaKohen (response defending restrictive interpretation)
Rambam (Maimonides' broader application of Cherev Ke'Chalal)
Tannaic debate: Rabbi (Judah HaNasi) vs. Rabbi Yosei bar Rabbi Yehuda
Box, wagon, boat, mast (modes of transport in Eretz Ha'amim)
Lo shechiach lav gazru (principle: rare cases exempt from rabbinic decrees)
Yotzei rosho v'rubo (leaning out head or majority of body)
